title: Enterprise AI with Microsoft Semantic Kernel

modules.title: Semantic Kernel Architecture: Plugins, Planners, and the Kernel Pipeline
modules.sub: Understanding the core building blocks of the Semantic Kernel SDK
modules.description: Learners explore how Semantic Kernel structures its runtime, tracing how requests flow through the kernel pipeline from prompt to response. They map out the roles of plugins and planners and decide how these components would fit into an existing application architecture. By the end, learners can articulate why each layer exists and what design choices the SDK enforces.

modules.title: Connecting LLMs to Enterprise Systems: Connectors, Memory, and Context Management
modules.sub: Wiring language models into databases, services, and organizational data
modules.description: Learners configure connectors to link Semantic Kernel with enterprise data sources and external services, then set up memory stores to persist and retrieve contextual information across interactions. They make deliberate decisions about what context to include, truncate, or summarize to stay within model limits while preserving relevance. The module produces a working integration between a language model and at least one enterprise backend.

modules.title: Building Reusable AI Plugins for Business Workflows
modules.sub: Designing modular, shareable AI capabilities for repeatable enterprise tasks
modules.description: Learners build semantic and native plugins that encapsulate discrete business logic, making them callable by the kernel and reusable across different workflows. They practice structuring plugin interfaces so that planners can discover and invoke them reliably, and they apply naming and documentation conventions that support team-wide reuse. The deliverable is a small plugin library aligned to a realistic business process.

modules.title: Orchestrating Multi-Step AI Tasks with Planners and Agents
modules.sub: Coordinating complex, goal-driven sequences of AI actions automatically
modules.description: Learners configure planners to decompose high-level goals into ordered sequences of plugin calls, then evaluate when agentic loops are more appropriate than static plans. They build and test a multi-step workflow that branches or retries based on intermediate outputs, observing how the kernel manages state between steps. This module gives learners direct control over the trade-offs between plan determinism and agent flexibility.

modules.title: Security, Governance, and Cost Control in Production Deployments
modules.sub: Enforcing organizational guardrails, access controls, and budget boundaries
modules.description: Learners apply authentication, authorization, and prompt filtering strategies to a Semantic Kernel deployment, then configure token usage policies to keep inference costs within acceptable thresholds. They evaluate governance patterns such as audit logging, responsible AI filters, and approval gates that align with enterprise compliance requirements. The module results in a hardened deployment configuration that could be reviewed by a security or compliance team.

modules.title: Integrating Semantic Kernel with Azure OpenAI and Microsoft 365
modules.sub: Connecting enterprise Microsoft services to a governed AI pipeline
modules.description: Learners wire Semantic Kernel to Azure OpenAI Service endpoints and configure the necessary authentication and deployment settings for a production tenant. They extend the integration to surface AI capabilities within Microsoft 365 contexts, such as processing data from SharePoint or triggering workflows via Graph API calls. The module produces an end-to-end connection between the kernel and the Microsoft cloud services most organizations already rely on.

modules.title: Testing, Observability, and Debugging AI Pipelines
modules.sub: Diagnosing, monitoring, and validating AI workflows before and after deployment
modules.description: Learners write unit and integration tests for plugins and planner outputs, accounting for the non-deterministic nature of LLM responses by designing assertions around structure and intent rather than exact strings. They instrument the kernel pipeline with logging and telemetry hooks, then use those signals to trace failures and unexpected behaviors in a running workflow. The module leaves learners with a repeatable quality assurance approach suited to AI-powered production systems.

modules.title: Applying Semantic Kernel to a Real Enterprise Use Case
modules.sub: Designing and delivering a complete, governed AI solution end to end
modules.description: Learners select or are assigned a realistic enterprise scenario and design a full Semantic Kernel solution that draws on every prior module, from plugin architecture through governance and observability. They build, test, and document the solution as they would for a real organizational stakeholder, making and justifying key architectural decisions along the way. The capstone produces an artifact that demonstrates production readiness and serves as a portfolio reference for professional contexts.

synopsis: Semantic Kernel is Microsoft's production-grade SDK for embedding LLM capabilities into enterprise applications — built for .NET, Python, and Java environments already common in large organizations. This course gives software engineers, architects, and technical leads a hands-on path from SDK fundamentals to deploying governed, scalable AI workflows inside real enterprise infrastructure.
rationale: As Microsoft deepens AI integration across its enterprise stack, Semantic Kernel is rapidly becoming the default orchestration layer for corporate AI development — professionals without this skill will cede technical leadership on AI initiatives.
learning_outcome: Learners will be able to design, build, and deploy a production-ready AI workflow using Semantic Kernel that integrates with existing enterprise systems and meets organizational governance requirements.
source_signals: Windows 11 adds AI agent that runs in background with access to personal folders (score: 2617)
